item.title: US still talking with countries to resettle Afghans as Rubio defends entry to white South Africans
item.description: Secretary of State Marco Rubio told lawmakers Tuesday that the U.S. is still in discussions with several countries to resettle more than 1,000 Afghans who assisted America’s war effort, while he defended the Trump administration's decision to green-light refugee admissions for tens of thousands of white South Africans. Democrats on House and Senate committees questioned Rubio during an annual budget hearing about why the U.S. has not followed through on its promise to take in the hundreds of allies who had been rigorously vetted before President Donald Trump signed executive orders in January 2025 that targeted asylum and refugee cases. “We’re obviously operating right now under a directive that prohibits the entry of Afghans into the United States,” Rubio said.
item.dc:creator: AP

item.title: Trump signs AI order after delay
item.description: Trump on Tuesday signed an executive order asking AI companies to give the government early access to their models.
item.dc:creator: Semafor

item.title: UN chief proposes options to help end Israel-Hezbollah conflict after peacekeepers leave Lebanon
item.description: The United Nations chief has proposed three options to help end the decades-old conflict between Lebanon’s Iran-backed Hezbollah militants and Israel when the 8,100-member U.N. peacekeeping mission in southern Lebanon ends on Dec. 31. All of the options presented to the U.N. Security Council by Secretary-General Antonio Guterres would continue U.N. military monitoring of the boundary between Israel and Lebanon, support Lebanese forces in deploying throughout the country and strengthen political efforts to end the fighting, which has persisted despite a nominal ceasefire.
item.dc:creator: AP

item.title: NHL's Dallas Stars plan move to suburbs in 5 years, with NBA's Mavs also leaving downtown then
item.description: The Dallas Stars are planning to move north, out of downtown and to the suburb of Plano in five years after the lease is up at the NHL team's current home arena. Stars officials announced Tuesday the signing of a nonbinding letter of intent to build a new hockey-specific arena and entertainment district about 20 miles north of the downtown American Airlines Center, which they have shared with the NBA’s Dallas Mavericks since it opened in 2001. The Stars made their intentions known a day after the Mavericks said they have a preliminary agreement on a site for their own new arena about 10 miles north of downtown, but still within the Dallas city limits.
item.dc:creator: AP

item.title: European Union observers reject Petro’s fraud claims, calling Colombia’s vote 'transparent'
item.description: The European Union’s Electoral Observation Mission to Colombia on Tuesday dismissed President Gustavo’s Petro’s claims of fraud in Sunday’s bitterly contested presidential election, saying the vote count was carried out in a “transparent, orderly and fluid” manner. The mission’s chief, Esteban González Pons, said none of the 12 candidates in Sunday’s election came to his mission with claims of irregularities. A second round of voting is scheduled for June 21 pitting lawyer Abelardo de la Espriella against Sen. Iván Cepeda, the candidates who garnered the most votes.

item.title: Bangladesh's foreign minister defeats Cyprus' ambassador to be UN General Assembly president
item.description: Bangladesh's Foreign Minister Khalilur Rahman won a hotly contested race Tuesday to be the next president of the 193-member United Nations General Assembly. In a secret-ballot vote, Rahman defeated Cyprus' Ambassador Andreas Kakouris 99-91, with three countries not voting. While the presidency of the General Assembly is largely ceremonial, it is also prestigious.

item.title: UK police handcuffed teen who died from stab wound in a case stirring race and policing debate
item.description: The fatal stabbing of a British teenager who was handcuffed by police while his killer stood nearby erupted into a debate Tuesday about policing, race and knife crime. The killing of Henry Nowak, 18, happened in December, but the story got renewed attention after the killer was sentenced to life with a minimum of 21 years in prison Monday, and video was released that showed police not believing Nowak when he said that he had been stabbed. The killer, Vickrum Digwa, 23, who is Sikh, had reported to police that he was the victim of a racist attack by Nowak, who was white.

item.title: Fear shadows Peru’s runoff vote as extortion and killings surge nationwide
item.description: In a desert area along northwestern Peru’s Pacific coast, Gladys Saavedra eyed with suspicion the strangers who arrive at the small market where she works alongside a group of women who, despite meager sales, must collectively give $300 a month to extortionists or risk paying an even higher price. The market in Trujillo was set on fire last June when the women refused to give in to threats. “You can’t even stick your head out for fear of being shot,” Saavedra, 49, said.

item.title: PHOTO ESSAY: Fear grips Peru as crime surges ahead of presidential runoff on Sunday
item.description: Trujillo mourned its dead, killed in crime waves that have targeted this Peruvian city. Small business owners struggle and extortion by gangs is rampant as in many other places across the South American country. Insecurity has emerged as a key concern ahead of a presidential runoff in Peru on Sunday, shaping people's daily routines and influencing how Peruvians see their country's future.

item.title: New York Democrats take first steps toward an aggressive 2028 redistricting plan
item.description: New York Democrats are wading into the national redistricting wars — just don't expect a victory before this year's midterm elections. Democrats in the state Legislature are taking the first steps toward a plan to give themselves more control over redistricting, introducing a proposal Monday that could open the door to a new set of aggressively gerrymandered congressional lines for the 2028 elections. The measure came as Democrats in New York vowed to counter Republican redistricting efforts elsewhere but were barred from redrawing the state's map without a constitutional amendment, which requires two legislative approvals over two years before it can go to voters as a ballot referendum.

item.title: Movie Review: ‘Masters of the Universe’ does not have the power
item.description: “Masters of the Universe” might not know who its audience is. Modern blockbusters are usually aiming for those golden four quadrants that might justify spending hundreds of millions of dollars to make a movie about an old toy. The animated series came the next year, and in 1987, a movie was released with Dolph Lundgren.

item.title: IDF strike damages hospital in Tyre, military says facility was not originally targeted
item.description: "It should be emphasized that the hospital was not targeted and was apparently damaged incidentally as a result of the strikes," the military clarified. An attack against Hezbollah terror infrastructure in Tyre on Monday caused damage to a civilian hospital in the area, the IDF said after reviewing footage from the strike.

item.title: Future presence of US troops in Lithuania is ‘under review,’ says minister
item.description: U.S. troops presently stationed in the country are now leaving as expected, but the next scheduled group is not arriving, the defense minister said.

item.title: Are Ukrainian Mirage 2000s Now Flying Air-To-Ground Strike Missions?
item.description: A low-level run followed by a steep climb hints at a ground-attack mission as part of the Mirage’s expanding combat repertoire.

item.title: EU plans largest ever wildfire response for 2026 summer
item.description: The EU is planning its largest-ever operation to combat forest fires this summer. In light of the increasing risk of wildfires across Europe, nearly 800 firefighters from 14 countries will be deployed to particularly vulnerable areas in Cyprus, Greece, Italy, France, Spain and Portugal, European Commission President Ursula von der Leyen said on Tuesday.

item.title: Roman Gofman named new head of Israel's Mossad intelligence service
item.description: Despite criticism surrounding his appointment, Roman Gofman has formally taken over as head of Israel's foreign intelligence agency, the Mossad. Israeli Prime Minister Benjamin Netanyahu nominated Gofman for the post.

item.title: Ivanka Trump, Jared Kushner Island Resort Project Under Scrutiny—Here’s Why
item.description: Jared Kushner's proposed luxury resort development in Albania is under growing scrutiny as protests escalate across the country.

item.title: Poland moves to ban smartphones in primary schools
item.description: Poland plans to ban mobile phones from all primary schools from the start of the next academic year under draft legislation approved by the government on Tuesday. The proposal, which will now be submitted to parliament, would take effect on September 1, 2026.

item.title: Tito Benady, seventh-generation Gibraltarian and leading local historian of the Rock
item.description: Tito Benady, who has died aged 95, divided his time between a farm in Northamptonshire and his native Gibraltar, where he was the leading local historian, proprietor of the Gibraltar Bookshop, publisher of Gibraltar Books and founding editor of the Gibraltar Heritage Journal.

item.title: British Police Officer Accused of Conning Dior, Cartier and Moncler in Brazen Refund Racket
item.description: Metropolitan Police officer pleads not guilty of cheating luxury stores and abusing computer systems for personal benefit. Ms. Sunna Harrison-Aziz, aged 28, made a plea of not guilty on Tuesday… The post British Police Officer Accused of Conning Dior, Cartier and Moncler in Brazen Refund Racket appeared first on Where Is The Buzz | Breaking News, Entertainment, Exclusive Interviews & More.

item.title: Brazil's Lula nods to China as Trump administration proposes 25% tariff
item.description: SAO PAULO, June 2 (Reuters) - Brazilian President Luiz Inacio Lula da Silva on Tuesday highlighted his country's ties with China after the U.S. proposed a new punitive tariff of 25% on many imports

item.title: Sweden Warns Parents: Your Phone Habits Are Hurting Your Kids
item.description: Sweden's Public Health Agency issues first guidelines targeting parents' phone habits, linking adult screen time to negative child development and behavioral modeling effects.

item.title: Lahav 433, Shin Bet file indictment to terrorist who held body of St.-Sgt. Shaul for 10 years
item.description: Ibrahim Hilo, the terrorist responsible for holding St. -Sgt.

item.title: Israel strikes southern Lebanon but partial truce with Hezbollah appears to hold
item.description: Israel continues its campaign in the south, though has not struck Beirut after a deal announced by the US.

item.title: Spain's future queen earns paratrooper badge
item.description: Spain’s Crown Princess Leonor has completed paratrooper training at the Méndez Parada Military Parachuting School at the Alcantarilla Air Base in Murcia region, the Royal Household announced in Madrid on Tuesday. Leonor, 20, has completed several jumps, including a night jump in May, according to media reports.

item.title: UN children's body warns on increased transport costs via Middle East
item.description: Tensions in the Middle East are increasing costs for providing humanitarian aid, Jean-Cédric Meeùs, the logistics head for UNICEF, the UN's children's organization, said in Geneva on Tuesday. Meeùs noted that many ships were having to take lengthy detours around the Cape of Good Hope, increasing the time at sea by up to four weeks.

item.title: Powerful Iran-backed militias in Iraq say they will start handing in weapons to authorities
item.description: Two of Iraq’s most powerful Iran-backed militias said on Tuesday they would begin handing in their weapons to the authorities, a major step in the new government’s effort to rein in militias that have long operated on their own even though they were nominally under state command. One of the groups, Asaib Ahl al-Haq, said it had formed a committee to oversee the move, inventory its fighters, weapons and equipment, and coordinate with the commander-in-chief of Iraq's armed forces. It cast the decision as a response to calls by Iraq’s top Shiite religious authority and the Iran-aligned Coordination Framework, the largest bloc in Parliament that dominates Iraqi politics.

item.title: Middle East war hammering aid supply chains: UN
item.description: Even if the Middle East war stopped immediately, disrupted global humanitarian supply lines would not recover before 2027, the United Nations said Tuesday.Nearly 100 days on from the February 28 US-Israeli attacks on Iran that triggered the conflict, the fall-out extends far beyond the Middle East region, said Jean-Cedric Meeus, chief of global transport and logistics for the UN children's agency UNICEF. "The disruption to the global humanitarian supply chain is impacting children across all the

item.title: Bahrain spy agency officer is sentenced to life over in-custody death
item.description: A court in Bahrain on Tuesday sentenced a member of the Gulf kingdom's domestic spy agency to life in prison over the in-custody death of a man arrested during the Iran war. Bahrain’s state news agency said the ruling came after investigators concluded that the unidentified National Intelligence Service officer was responsible for the man’s death. The public prosecutor's special investigative unit did not mention the man him by name, but the date of the death lines up with Mohamed al-Mousawi, a 32-year-old Shiite Muslim whose body was returned to his family bearing bruises, burns and cuts, witnesses who saw him at the morgue and his funeral said. A forensic expert with Physicians for Human Rights told The Associated Press that his injuries matched descriptions of blunt force trauma and torture.

item.title: Istanbul's 'Sunken Palace' closed to tourists after court ruling
item.description: Tourists are being turned away from one of Istanbul's most popular attractions, the Basilica Cistern, after a court ruling transferred control of the historic site away from the city's opposition-led administration, according to local media. The ruling means the site is now operated by the General Directorate of Foundations, which falls under the Ministry of Culture and Tourism, governed by the ruling AKP party, the state news agency Anadolu reported on Tuesday.

item.title: Henry Nowak: The murder that exposed Britain’s two-tier justice system
item.description: Camilla Tominey and Tim Stanley examine the harrowing details and body-cam footage of Henry Nowak’s murder and debate what the tragic case reveals about Britain’s “two-tier” policing system.

item.title: Chile's birth rate falls to historic low, raising concerns
item.description: Chile has recorded a historic decline in births, with the birth rate falling 46.9% over the past 32 years and dropping below one child per woman.

item.title: Zimbabwe justice minister introduces bill to extend 83-year-old president's term
item.description: Zimbabwe’s justice minister on Tuesday introduced a constitutional amendment bill in Parliament that would extend the tenure of the country’s 83-year-old president and shift presidential elections from a direct popular vote to selection by lawmakers. The proposal would defer elections due in 2028 by two years and extend President Emmerson Mnangagwa’s term to 2030. Justice Minister Ziyambi Ziyambi last week said he aims to complete the legislative process and have the measure passed by Parliament by the end of June, after which Mnangagwa can sign it into law.

item.title: Look: Expert called to catch loose porcupine recognizes his own pet
item.description: An exotic animal expert called in to help capture a porcupine on the loose in Poland was surprised to discover the loose animal was his own pet.

item.title: 'Life and hope': Lebanon hospital resilient after Israeli attack
item.description: In a south Lebanon hospital heavily damaged by deadly Israeli strikes nearby, Dr Nasser al-Masri held a new-born baby, calling him "a message of life and hope" despite the war.He brought us a message of life and a message of hope for the future."

item.title: British couple lose Iran jail sentence appeal, family says
item.description: Lindsay and Craig Foreman were arrested in January 2025 during a global motorbike journey.

item.title: Woman 'Played Dead' as Housemate Allegedly Strangled Her, Then 'Screamed' for Help in SOS Call from Cellphone
item.description: Donatas Jankauskas, 44, has been charged with assault, with police alleging in court that he was intoxicated and aggressive

item.title: Iran plans funeral for slain supreme leader Ali Khamenei
item.description: More than three months after Iran's former supreme leader Ayatollah Ali Khamenei was killed in an Israeli airstrike, officials have for the first time commented publicly on plans for his burial. The funeral is scheduled to take place at the start of the Islamic month of mourning, Muharram, Iranian media quoted the deputy mayor of Tehran, Amin Tavakolizadeh, as saying on Tuesday.

item.title: Local party chief probed after 82 killed in Chinese mining tragedy
item.description: BEIJING, June 2 (Reuters) - A party chief in the northern coal-rich Chinese province of Shanxi is under investigation over "suspected serious violations of discipline and law", the local anti-graft

item.title: US woman influencer cautioned after admitting London assault
item.description: A US woman influencer with ties to President Donald Trump's MAGA movement has admitted to assaulting a woman at a London Tube station last year, a court was told Tuesday.The PR consultant vied for the job of White House spokesperson for Trump's second term, but the president ultimately picked long-time loyalist Karoline Leavitt.

item.title: More black men to benefit from prostate cancer screening trial
item.description: Experts are trying to find the best way to screen for prostate cancer, since blood tests alone are not accurate enough for most men.

item.title: South Africa police investigate killing of two Mozambican men
item.description: The deaths come after a day of violent protests against illegal migration in the town of Mossel Bay.

item.title: Expulsion of NYT reporter shows China's dual-track visa strategy
item.description: China is severely limiting visas for foreign reporters — who often dig into the dark side of life there — while offering visa-free entry for tourists and Western influencers.

item.title: Companies and countries grapple with prospect of Hormuz toll
item.description: The CEO of Chevron said he will not consider paying a toll, but Qatar appeared to be more flexible.

item.title: At Least 7 Killed After Tourist Jeep Plummets Into Ravine While Traveling to Vacation Hotspot: Reports
item.description: A rescue operation was launched, but first responders were reportedly unable to save the passengers

item.title: British couple jailed for ‘spying on Iran’ lose appeal
item.description: A British couple jailed on spying charges in Iran have lost an appeal against their convictions, their family has said.

item.title: Sweden weighs social media age limit of 15
item.description: Sweden should introduce a minimum age of 15 for the use ‌of social media, a government-appointed commission recommended on ‌Tuesday. • Several European nations are seeking to rein in children's ​use of social media after Australia took the lead with a world-first ban on under-16s in December last year. • "The reasons for introducing an age limit nevertheless outweigh ‌the benefits of ⁠continued free access to this type of media," Swedish investigator Lisa Englund Krafft ⁠told a news conference with Social Affairs and Public Health Minister Jakob Forssmed.

item.title: Stop getting drunk on your flight — or risk landing on the UK's rowdy passenger database
item.description: Intoxicated travelers causing trouble are a "real challenge for all airlines," Ryanair CEO Michael O'Leary said in May.

item.title: UN warns funding cuts could worsen Rohingya crisis in Bangladesh
item.description: Source: Reuters

item.title: Kenya protests over US Ebola centre turn deadly
item.description: Protests over a fiercely contested Ebola quarantine centre for US nationals in Kenya have killed one man, a rights group said Tuesday, as President William Ruto defended the planned centre.Hussein Khalid of rights group VOCAL Africa said in an X post that a 27-year-old man was "shot and killed" during the protests and "died on the spot".

item.title: Europe plans to streamline deportations of unauthorized migrants
item.description: The new legislation will allow deportation hubs outside the bloc’s borders.

item.title: Mozambique says five citizens killed in S.Africa 'xenophobic attacks'
item.description: South African police said Tuesday two Mozambicans were killed in violence at the weekend but refused to confirm the Mozambique government's announcement that five of its citizens had died in "xenophobic attacks".The South African police only confirmed that two Mozambicans, aged 27 and 43, had died after being assaulted following the march.

item.title: London hit by severe travel disruption as underground drivers strike
item.description: London's "tube" train network was thrown into chaos after 1,700 drivers walked off the job, forcing the transit authority to shut down several lines.

item.title: Israel, Hezbollah continue to trade fire despite U.S.-brokered truce
item.description: A partial cease-fire brokered by Donald Trump failed to stop fighting between Israel and Hezbollah in southern Lebanon and across the Israeli border.

item.title: Germany's Merz calls for de-escalation in Lebanon
item.description: Germany views the latest escalation of hostilities between Israel and Hezbollah in Lebanon "with great concern," Chancellor Friedrich Merz said on Tuesday. Merz called on Hezbollah to "immediately stop attacking the people of Israel, and to lay down its arms," while demanding that Israel adhere to the ceasefire agreed in April.

item.title: Woman Wins Year-Long Court Battle Fighting Unfair Seatbelt Ticket
item.description: One Australian family’s fight over a roughly $1,200 AI-detected seatbelt fine has ended in a win after a year-long court battle. The case hinged on whether the family had been wearing seatbelts, but on whether ... Read more

item.title: Legendary 130-year-old French wine restored after decades under Czech castle floor
item.description: Eight bottles of a legendary French wine that survived World War II and decades of communist rule hidden under a Czech castle floor have been lovingly restored by the chateau that produced them some 130 years ago.The wine spent decades hidden under the floorboards of the castle chapel alongside the shrine of St Maurus before communist secret police found them in 1985.

item.title: UNHCR: No objection to EU's deportation centres in third countries
item.description: The UN refugee organization is not opposed to deportation centres in third countries for asylum seekers rejected by member states of the European Union, UNHCR spokesman Barbar Baloch said in Geneva on Tuesday. Baloch was speaking about Monday's decision by representatives of the European Parliament and the EU-member state governments on so-called "return hubs.

item.title: Anthropic offers EU access to Mythos
item.description: Mythos was unveiled but not released in April after it was found to be able to detect — and exploit — security flaws in almost all systems.

item.title: American soldier and British soldier die in training accident in Iraq
item.description: The U.S. and British militaries say one American soldier and one British soldier died during a training exercise in Iraq.

item.title: Russia slams key Ukrainian cities in one of deadliest offensives in months
item.description: Moscow unleashed a lethal barrage on Ukraine early Tuesday, hitting the capital Kyiv and the central city of Dnipro in a broad-ranging offensive that inflicted one of the deadliest attack for months.

item.title: Italy marks 80 years as a republic with military parade in Rome
item.description: Italy marked the 80th anniversary of the founding of the republic with a military parade through central Rome on Tuesday. President Sergio Mattarella laid a wreath at the Altar of the Fatherland on Rome's Piazza Venezia, as a flight of aircraft laid trails in the green, white and red of the national flag.

item.title: Some 20 injured in serious tram accident in Berlin
item.description: Twenty people have been injured when a tram derailed in eastern Berlin, the fire brigade said on Tuesday. Three people sustained serious injuries in the accident in Berlin's Lichtenberg district, a spokesman said.

item.title: Israel's weapons exports reach a record high with sales doubling in the past 5 years
item.description: Israeli weapons exports reached a record high of more than $19 billion last year, a 30% increase from 2024, Israel's Defense Ministry said Tuesday. More than half of 2025’s sales were “mega-deals” valued at $100 million or more, the ministry said, adding that sales have more than doubled in the last five years, despite widespread criticism of Israel’s conduct in its wars in Gaza, with Hezbollah and with Iran. Countries that have vowed to shun Israeli weapons makers are nonetheless quietly placing orders, according to industry officials.

item.title: Cambodia initiates action with UN agency to force conciliation of maritime dispute with Thailand
item.description: Cambodia's government has filed notice under a U.N. agreement on maritime law for compulsory conciliation of a sea border dispute with neighboring Thailand, Prime Minister Hun Manet said Tuesday. The decision to take the matter to the the U.N. Convention on the Law of the Sea, or UNCLOS, comes after Thailand last month terminated a 25-year-old memorandum of understanding with Cambodia meant to resolve overlapping maritime claims.

item.title: 9 students appear in court over Kenya school arson that killed 16 girls
item.description: Nine students accused of planning and executing an arson attack that killed 16 girls at a school in central Kenya appeared in court Tuesday. The High Court in the town of Naivasha, 90 kilometers (55 miles) west of the capital, Nairobi, said it will issue a ruling on Wednesday on whether the girls could be detained for a month pending investigations. The fire on May 28 ripped through the Utumishi Girls School dormitory that houses 202 students.

item.title: Violence against migrants in South Africa worsens
item.description: Mozambique said five of its citizens were killed in anti-migrant attacks across the country.

item.title: Russia launches barrage of strikes on Ukraine
item.description: The 700-plus drones and missiles came after it fired more than 1,500 over two days last month.

item.title: Iran studying deal to halt war as stalemate persists
item.description: DUBAI, June 2 (Reuters) - Iran is reviewing a proposed agreement with the U. to halt their war, Iranian media reported on Tuesday, after U.

item.title: Man given life sentence for murdering his mother
item.description: Annie Heyneman was murdered at her home in County Cavan in January 2025

item.title: The Current Ebola Outbreak Is A Global Threat. A Doctor Explains
item.description: The Ebola outbreak has already spread to Uganda and threatens neighboring states such as war-ravaged South Sudan. It is rapidly becoming a major global health threat.

item.title: Syrian on trial in Germany for injuring two with axe on train
item.description: A 21-year-old Syrian man accused of attacking passengers with an axe on a long-distance train went on trial in Germany on Tuesday. The man injured two people on an ICE express train on July 3, 2025 when it was travelling near the south-eastern town of Strasskirchen, according to investigators.

item.title: Poland to ban phones in schools, restrict access to pornography
item.description: WARSAW, June 2 (Reuters) - Poland wants to ban under-16s from using mobile phones in schools from September 1 and plans to introduce stricter age verification rules to access pornography, Prime

item.title: 'Some kind of apocalypse': A Kyiv resident recalls terror of Russian attack
item.description: KYIV, June 2 (Reuters) - When a large explosion shook Olha Mudra's apartment block in Kyiv in the early hours of Tuesday, it felt like the end of ‌the world. Mudra, her hair covered in dust and face smeared with soot, recalled the moment when the ‌blast occurred, in the third mass attack on the Ukrainian capital in as many weeks.

item.title: The Chagas disease time bomb in Latin America’s cities
item.description: Sometime in the next decade, a woman in Buenos Aires will go to her cardiologist complaining of breathlessness and fatigue.

item.title: Woman falls into broken manhole
item.description: A woman in Brazil fell through a manhole that had been broken by cable thieves, CCTV footage showed.

item.title: Ghana's anti-LGBTQ+ bill to be scrutinised before approval, president says
item.description: Passed by MPs, the bill proposes prison sentences for people identifying as part of the LGBTQ+ community.

item.title: WHO says suspected Ebola cases drop to 116 after hundreds ruled out
item.description: By Olivia Le Poidevin and Jennifer Rigby GENEVA, June 2 (Reuters) - The World Health Organization said there have been 321 confirmed cases of Ebola in the Democratic Republic of Congo outbreak and 116

item.title: Maga influencer admits Bond Street assault
item.description: Melissa Rein Lively accepted a caution, while an assault by beating charge against her was withdrawn.

item.title: The ceasefires in Gaza, Lebanon and Iran are stretching the term's meaning
item.description: Ceasefires have been announced, often to great fanfare, in Gaza, Lebanon and Iran. In just the last few weeks, Israeli forces have captured more territory in Gaza and killed two top Hamas militants there, as well as more than a dozen other people. In Lebanon, Israeli troops captured a Crusader fortress over the weekend in their deepest incursion in 26 years, as Hezbollah kept up rocket fire into northern Israel.

item.title: Bear goes on rampage in Japan, injuring 4 people
item.description: A record 13 people were killed by bears in Japan last year, and there has been a jump in sightings as the animals emerge hungry from hibernation.

item.title: Uganda health ministry confirms six new cases of Ebola
item.description: KAMPALA, June 2 (Reuters) - Uganda has confirmed six more new cases of Ebola, bringing the total confirmed in the country so far to 15, the health ministry said on Tuesday.

item.title: Pope names Mexican-American broadcasting executive to lead Vatican communications
item.description: Pope Leo XIV took a step Tuesday toward overhauling the Vatican’s communications operations by naming the Mexican-American president of the Catholic U.S. media conglomerate EWTN News as its new head. Maria Montserrat Alvarado replaces Paolo Ruffini as prefect of the Dicastery of Communications, the office that controls the Vatican’s television, radio, online, publishing and newspaper operations.

item.title: Cuba Faces New Catastrophe As Key European Airline Stops Direct Flights
item.description: Nearly a dozen airlines have cancelled or restricted flights to Cuba since the start of the year.

item.title: Genital herpes rising in England, despite overall drop in STIs
item.description: New cases of genital herpes have increased, while many other sexually transmitted infections (STIs) have started to decline.

item.title: Iran's inflation hits World War II levels, deepening economic pain
item.description: Year-on-year inflation in Iran reached a level in May unseen since World War II, underlining the economic pain average Iranians face as the Islamic Republic worries about the war with Israel and the United States restarting. A report Monday by Iran's Central Bank represents the first official acknowledgment of what Iranians shopping, paying for a taxi or visiting a medical clinic already know: The rial currency is battered by the war and uncertainty around it resuming. Meanwhile, longtime problems of economic mismanagement and government corruption also appear to be dragging down Iran's oil-backed economy as it remains under a U.S. naval blockade.

item.title: German left-wing MPs slam EU deal on return hubs in third countries
item.description: German left-wing lawmakers on Tuesday criticized an EU deal that has cleared the way for the establishment of deportation centres outside the bloc, describing the plans as inhumane. The deal was reached on Monday evening by representatives of the European Parliament and the governments of EU countries.

item.title: Two detained in Italy after four bodies found in burnt-out car
item.description: Two men have been detained following the discovery of the charred bodies of four men in a burnt-out car at an Italian petrol station, the Italian news agency ANSA reported on Tuesday, citing the public prosecutor's office responsible for the case. The men are suspected of quadruple murder and were taken into custody after several hours of questioning by police, ANSA reported.

item.title: Israel kills five in attacks on Lebanon after Trump announces de-escalation
item.description: The deadly attacks raise concerns about the reported agreement on the cessation of hostilities.

item.title: How social media is turning African life into content at a cost
item.description: Across Africa, social media redefines politics, culture, identity, and connection, blurring community and performance.

item.title: Eastern Europe’s aristocrats still need their property back
item.description: During the mid-20th century, Eastern Europe’s communist regimes carried out what was, perhaps, the largest real-estate theft in history.

item.title: How the war has made Iran’s water crisis worse
item.description: Attacks on desalination plants and other water infrastructure by US and Israel have further added to the water woes.

item.title: Drones Have Not Replaced Artillery, But They Make It Harder To Fire
item.description: Drones dominate losses on the Russia-Ukraine battlefield, but they have not replaced artillery. Instead, they have forced both sides to adapt how artillery is employed.

item.title: As Congo grapples with Ebola, volunteers cook up meals to support patients and health workers
item.description: Arlette Basekawike, a volunteer for the United Nations food agency in Bunia, the heart of Congo's Ebola outbreak, spends most of her time in a small shed outside a health facility preparing meals for patients and health workers. “Even though the patients have this disease, they still feel better when they eat, and the doctors have the energy to treat the sick and give them medication,” Basekawike told The Associated Press on Monday as she prepared vegetables and potatoes with goat meat in a large cooking pot.

item.title: Two people shot dead amid Kenya protests against US Ebola quarantine centre plan
item.description: The US plan has sparked public anger and led to demonstrations near the site of the proposed treatment facility.

item.title: Australia’s ACCC directs four retail platforms to delist potentially dangerous toys
item.description: The takedown orders target listings for toys that include small high-powered magnets covered by a permanent prohibition under Australian Consumer Law in place since 2012.

item.title: High Court orders Kenya government to release details of Ebola facility
item.description: Hundreds of people took to the streets in the central Kenyan town of Nanyuki to protest against the planned site.

item.title: Protests over US Ebola site in Kenya kill two, court keeps block
item.description: By Humphrey Malalo NAIROBI, June 2 (Reuters) - A Kenyan court blocked on Tuesday for another three weeks a proposed U.S. Ebola quarantine facility that has triggered protests killing two people.

item.title: Reports of discrimination in Germany rise to record high
item.description: A record number of people contacted Germany's anti-discrimination agency last year, with most reporting having suffered from racist discrimination, according to new figures released on Tuesday. Germany's federal anti-discrimination agency offers support to people who have experienced discrimination on the grounds of race or ethnic origin, gender, religion or belief, disability, age or sexual identity.

item.title: More than 50% of German adults are overweight, new figures show
item.description: More than half of German adults are overweight, according to official figures released on Tuesday, with men particularly affected. Some 53% of adults aged 18 or older surveyed in 2025 had a body mass index (BMI) of over 25, the Federal Statistical Office said.

item.title: Drone racers might seem like ideal combat pilots. But trainers say they have to relearn almost everything.
item.description: Ukraine's drone operators often have to fly slowly or have their drone wait a long time before attacking. That's a different approach than racing.

item.title: Congo re-opens airport at centre of Ebola outbreak
item.description: KINSHASA, June 2 (Reuters) - Democratic Republic of Congo has re-opened the airport in the capital city of the province hit hardest by the ongoing Ebola outbreak, a government statement said,

item.title: Deadly Russian strikes rock Kyiv as Zelenskyy appeals to US for urgent help
item.description: Ukraine's air force said Russia launched 73 missiles and 656 drones.

item.title: U.S. soldier, British servicemember die during Iraq military training
item.description: A U.S. soldier and a British servicemember died during military training in northern Iraq over the weekend, both militaries said.

item.title: Iran executes three convicted rapists amid surge in capital punishment
item.description: Iran has executed three men convicted of rape, the Mizan news agency reported on Tuesday, amid a broader wave of executions that has drawn criticism from human rights groups. Two death sentences were carried out on Tuesday morning in the Kurdish city of Qorveh, while another was hanged in the northern city of Rasht, according to the judiciary-affiliated news agency.

item.title: Germany pledges another €20 million in aid for Lebanon
item.description: Germany has promised another €20 million ($23 million) in humanitarian aid to Lebanon, after Development Minister Reem Alabali Radovan had to cancel a planned visit to the country over security concerns amid ongoing Israeli attacks. “I pledged the €20 million aid package yesterday,” Alabali Radovan told Germany's Deutschlandfunk broadcaster on Tuesday, noting the long-standing partnership with Lebanon in development cooperation.

item.title: Israel, Hezbollah exchange fire after Trump announcement
item.description: Israel conducted strikes on south Lebanon and Hezbollah fired into northern Israel on Tuesday, after US President Donald Trump announced an agreement to halt attacks that neither side has publicly accepted.According to Lebanon's National News Agency, Israel conducted fresh air strikes on south Lebanon on Tuesday.

item.title: Historic Highland fortress damaged by vandals
item.description: Vandalism at Old Inverlochy Castle near Fort William could affect ongoing conservation work.

item.title: Pope Leo plays tennis religiously as part of an Augustinian devotion to sports and spirituality
item.description: When Pope Leo XIV was elected last year and it emerged that he was a tennis player, his love of the sport was quickly celebrated during an audience with top-ranked Jannik Sinner. In the ensuing months, Leo has tried to set aside time in his busy schedule to play the sport every week as part of his Augustinian devotion to physical activity and spirituality. The Rule of St. Augustine, an ancient guide for religious life, highlights the value of good habits.

item.title: 94 days of paralysis: The Strait of Hormuz remains choked off
item.description: The most powerful shipping executives in the world are gathered in Athens, Greece this week for the annual International Shipping Exhibition. The hot topic: the Strait of Hormuz.

item.title: Mozambique says 5 citizens killed in ‘xenophobic attacks’ in South Africa
item.description: The violence prompted 300 Mozambicans to return home over the weekend.

item.title: China goes after 'ghost kitchens' to rein in cut-throat food delivery apps
item.description: The thousands of "ghost kitchens" - online shops that don't actually exist - have spooked Chinese consumers.

item.title: Russia kills 13, injures more than 100 in overnight Ukraine attack
item.description: Russia launched massive drone-and-missile attack overnight, killing at least nine people and injuring dozens more in Dnipro, Kyiv and Kharkiv.

item.title: Kenyan president defends US Ebola quarantine center amid protests
item.description: Kenya’s president on Monday defended the establishment of an Ebola quarantine facility by the U.S., a move that led to protests despite a court order blocking the plan. President William Ruto on Monday night said the U.S. had a long-standing partnership with Kenya on health matters and that the quarantine facility at Laikipia Air Base was one of 24 facilities that had been established in the event of an Ebola outbreak in the country.

item.title: Satellite Image Shows Possible Chinese Structure on Disputed Reef
item.description: Scarborough Shoal, a rich fishing ground, continues to be a focal point in the Philippines' territorial feud with China.

item.title: Two Taiwanese air force pilots die in trainer crash
item.description: Two Taiwanese air force pilots died on Tuesday when their propeller-driven T-34 training aircraft crashed in Kaohsiung City. The T-34 trainer crashed shortly after 8 am (midnight GMT) north of the Gangshan Air Force Base, the state-owned Central News Agency (CNA) reported.

item.title: Major Russian attack hits Kiev, other Ukrainian cities
item.description: Russia has again launched a major missile and drone attack on the Ukrainian capital Kiev and other parts of the country, killing at least 10 people and injuring almost 100 others, Ukrainian officials said early on Tuesday. Powerful explosions were heard across central Kiev overnight.

item.title: Eleven killed as Russia fires hypersonic missiles at Ukraine
item.description: Russia bombarded Ukraine with a barrage of hypersonic missiles and drones overnight, killing at least 11 people, injuring dozens and trapping others.

item.title: Kyiv hit by deadly Russian strikes, people feared trapped in apartment building rubble

item.description: When Israeli soldiers seized Beaufort Castle in southern Lebanon on Sunday, they probably did not reflect on how its craggy Crusader battlements once witnessed a disastrous confidence trick.

item.description: DeepState UA said it was Russia's first month of net losses since 2023, when Ukraine launched its major summer offensive with Western arms.

item.title: UK's Cooper: Rules-based order in shared interest of Britain and China
item.description: The rules-based international order is in Britain and China's "shared interest," UK Foreign Secretary Yvette Cooper said as she met the country's Vice President Han Zheng for talks on global security as part of a three-day visit to Asia. Cooper acknowledged "areas of disagreement" between London and Beijing but insisted that approaching discussions with "candour and respect" would help to increase mutual understanding of one another.

item.title: Baerbock says UN reform overdue but world body remains indispensable
item.description: UN General Assembly President Annalena Baerbock called for bold reforms of the United Nations, saying the world body remains indispensable despite mounting criticism and growing geopolitical tensions. "Reform is long overdue.

item.title: Between ballet and war: Japanese dancers pursue dreams in Russia
item.description: En pointe, demi-pointe, entrechat -- for Jotaro Kanazasi and Haruka Takemi, two Japanese dancers based in Russia's historic city of Nizhny Novgorod, life revolves entirely around their art, even as the war in Ukraine occasionally interrupts their daily routine.Absorbed in their lives as professional dancers, Takemi and Kanazasi admit they follow the news only loosely, including the war Moscow has been waging in Ukraine since February 2022.

item.title: Fears of hunger overwhelm Guatemalan village as El Nino approaches
item.description: While drought expands through Cunen as the specter of El Nino climate instability approaches, one fear has seized this indigenous Guatemalan village: death by hunger. Her fears are well-founded: around 2.5 million Guatemalans face potential food insecurity due to the drought and the high probability of a powerful El Nino weather cycle.

item.title: 'I gave birth in the street': Conflict makes childbirth risky in parts of Africa
item.description: The agony began for Maude Ahmad Fadala shortly after sunset. There were no camp facilities for what was about to happen, and she had no money to travel. This is part of a series on maternal mortality in sub-Saharan Africa, which has the world’s fastest-growing population and the majority of mothers dying from pregnancy-related causes: 70%, or around 182,000 deaths every year.

item.title: Russian attack on Ukraine kills at least 16 and traps others in damaged buildings
item.description: Russia launched hundreds of drones and dozens of missiles against Kyiv and other Ukrainian cities overnight, killing at least 16 civilians and wounding more than 100 others, authorities said Tuesday. The damage trapped some people under the rubble of apartment buildings. Kyiv residents had been on edge for days after Russia warned that a massive aerial attack was coming and warned foreign diplomats to leave the Ukrainian capital.

item.title: Russian strikes rock Ukraine, killing nine and wounding dozens
item.description: A barrage of Russian missile and drone strikes killed nine people and wounded dozens across Ukraine, authorities said on Tuesday, just days after President Volodymyr Zelensky warned that Moscow was preparing a "massive new strike".The strikes on Ukraine came after Zelensky said on Friday "we have intelligence information about Russia preparing a new massive strike" and called for people to "protect your lives".

item.title: Survey: Most new German citizens keep original nationality
item.description: The vast majority of people who became German citizens last year retained their original nationality under Germany's new dual citizenship rules, according to a survey by the Mediendienst Integration research group. The share of newly naturalized citizens holding more than one nationality ranged from 85% to 98% in cities that collected the data, the survey found.

item.title: Anti-Labor critics riled up by false claim Australian flag banned on corrections officers' uniforms
item.description: Corrections Victoria says no flags are permitted on prison officers' uniforms, contradicting a state opposition lawmaker's false claim that the guards are barred from wearing the Australian flag while the Aboriginal and LGBTQ flags are allowed. The department told AFP the uniforms include the Victoria State Crest, and they fly the national flag outside every prison.

item.title: Vital to keep a UN force in Lebanon after current peacekeepers depart: Guterres
item.description: UN chief Antonio Guterres said Monday that peacekeepers will be needed in Lebanon after the mandate of the current mission expires at year-end -- an option likely to face opposition from the United States and Israel.Last August, the UN Security Council, under US pressure, decided to end the mandate of the United Nations Interim Force in Lebanon (UNIFIL) on December 31, 2026.

item.title: Australia says wheat crop set to plunge
item.description: Australia's wheat crop is set to fall by more than a quarter this season, a government report said Tuesday, as farmers face dry conditions and a surge in fuel and fertiliser prices fed by conflict in the Middle East.Farmers were expected to cut the total area planted with wheat by 12 percent, it said, citing lower profitability compared to other crops and "very dry" conditions in northern regions.

item.title: Powerful magnitude 6.2 earthquake rattles parts of Southern Italy

item.title: Powerful earthquake strikes off Italy's southern coast
item.description: A powerful earthquake struck off the coast of Italy's Calabria region early on Tuesday and was felt as far away as Naples, news agency ANSA reported. The earthquake's epicentre was in the Tyrrhenian Sea off the city of Cosenza, some 240 kilometres south-east of Naples.

item.title: Canada is failing the Jewish community and Jews are being targeted, Prime Minister Carney says
item.description: Canada is failing Jewish Canadians and the community is being brutally targeted by hate, Prime Minister Mark Carney said Monday. Carney said across Canada, antisemitism has surged to levels not seen in the post-World War II era.

item.description: An Iraqi national accused of plotting at least 18 attacks in Europe in retaliation for the U.S. and Israel’s war with Iran pleaded not guilty on Monday before calling himself a “prisoner of war” and telling a judge that children and women were being killed “by your rockets.” Mohammad Baqer Saad Dawood Al-Saadi finally was persuaded to sit down in Manhattan federal court as two marshals approached him at a judge's urging. Al-Saadi did not appear to be trying to be disruptive as he commented beyond his response to the charges.

item.title: Peruvian shamans perform a blessing ritual ahead of a presidential runoff
item.description: Peruvian shamans gathered on Monday for a blessing ritual for the two candidates in the country’s presidential runoff next weekend, a decisive vote for the South American country that has seen a revolving door of presidents kicked out of office over corruption scandals. The two — Keiko Fujimori, the conservative daughter of disgraced former President Alberto Fujimori, and Roberto Sánchez, a nationalist congressman and former minister — are neck and neck in the polls and will face off on Sunday.

item.title: German shopkeeper convicted over display of anti-Semitic sign
item.description: A man in Germany has been given a six-month suspended sentence for displaying an anti-Semitic notice in his shop. The court in the northern German city of Flensburg ruled on Monday that in September last year, the 60-year-old displayed a notice in a clearly visible position in his second-hand shop for around four hours which included the phrase "Jews are banned from these premises!!!" As a condition of his probation, he must pay a fine of €1,200 ($1,390) to the Ladelund Concentration Camp Memori

item.title: Abiy expected to retain power in Ethiopia election
item.description: Prime Minister Abiy Ahmed has pursued economic liberalization during his two terms in power.

item.title: Jewish woman wearing Star of David pendant forced out of Spanish sauna
item.description: A Jewish woman wearing a Star of David pendant was forced to leave a sauna in Barcelona, in what Israel has called an “anti-Semitic attack”.

item.title: UK Military Looks At Allowing Lethal Strikes With Zero Human Intervention
item.description: UK military officials suggest removing human control from lethal AI targeting in exceptional circumstances despite policy denying autonomous weapons development.

item.title: Ebola may have been spreading unchecked since January, aid groups fear
item.description: The first Ebola case in the current epidemic in the Democratic Republic of Congo (DRC) may date back as far as January, according to local medics.

item.title: African officials seek homegrown response to Ebola outbreak
item.description: Health systems in Africa are stretched, but leadership across the continent is driving the response, following major foreign aid cuts last year.

item.title: UN Security Council to hold emergency meeting on Lebanon
item.description: The UN Security Council is set to hold an emergency meeting later on Monday to discuss the escalating conflict in Lebanon, diplomats in New York said. The session was scheduled at the request of France and is due to begin at 3 pm (1900 GMT), according to diplomatic sources.

The archive, which may have contained explosive revelations, was passed on to Lord Chamberlain, the most senior official […] The post Buckingham Palace Allegedly Aware of Ex-Prince Andrew’s 2020 Trade Dealings appeared first on Mandatory.

item.title: Iran executes two men over January mosque arson amid trial outcry
item.description: Mehrdad Mohammadinia and Ashkan Maleki had allegedly been part of a group of demonstrators who targeted the Jafari Mosque in Tehran's Gisha neighborhood. The Islamic regime executed two men on Monday who were alleged to have burned a mosque during the January protests, the Iranian judicial outlet Mizan news agency reported.

item.description: Myanmar opposition groups criticise India for hosting Min Aung Hlaing, but India says engagement is best way forward.

item.description: “They tried to get the baby, but they couldn’t get to him,” Tracie Long said of firefighters' attempts to rescue her 3-year-old grandson

item.title: Israel urges south Beirut residents to leave as strikes intensify
item.description: The Israeli military has called on residents of the southern suburbs of the Lebanese capital Beirut to flee. In a message in Arabic on X, a military spokesman said that people should leave their homes for their own safety.

item.title: Germany sees lowest level of rubbish since 2009
item.description: Germans threw away less waste in 2024, with the total volume falling to its lowest level since 2009, the country's Federal Statistical Office reported on Monday. Preliminary results show that 362.

item.title: Egypt’s new monorail offers a modern ride, but Cairo is still not convinced
item.description: Cairo’s East Nile monorail opened to passengers this month as a flagship of Egypt’s infrastructure ambitions.

item.title: Several figures emerge as next leaders of Hamas in Gaza, but who will end up leading the group?
item.description: Although Hamas only controls half of Gaza, it still runs the lives of 2 million civilians and seeks out new leadership amid targeted eliminations by the IDF. Israel eliminated Hamas’ latest leader in Gaza, Mohammed Odeh, on May 26, just two weeks after the IDF had also killed his predecessor, Izz al-Din al-Haddad.

item.title: What is the British military actually for?
item.description: Defence debates have too long sidestepped the central question of what the British military is actually for. What purpose are the Armed Forces meant to serve? Who are they intended to fight? Where? With which allies? For how long? And to what end?

item.title: Iranian missiles target U.S. base in Kuwait as both sides keep trading fire
item.description: The U.S. military shot down Iranian missiles targeting American forces in Kuwait on Sunday night. Several U.S. troops were reportedly injured in similar strikes last week.

item.title: Former Syrian general under al-Assad faces torture trial in Vienna
item.description: Two senior security officials from the former Syrian government of ousted ruler Bashar al-Assad are on trial in Austria, accused of torture, grievous bodily harm and sexual assault, the prosecution at Vienna's regional court said on Monday. The defendants allegedly mistreated detainees themselves and gave orders for others to do so, the prosecutor said.

item.title: Docs Tell Woman to Take Painkillers for a Cold. After She's Found 'Barely Breathing,' She's Diagnosed with Meningitis

item.title: Ex-Prince Andrew under investigation after incident at Royal Ascot
item.description: Andrew Mountbatten-Windsor is in hot water once again. According to The Sunday Times, authorities are investigating the former Duke of York over an alleged encounter with a woman at Royal Ascot in 2002.

item.title: Norway’s Crown Prince Haakon Cuts Japan Trip Short as His Wife’s Health Continues to Deteriorate
item.description: Crown Prince Haakon is returning early from an official trip to Japan as Crown Princess Mette-Marit’s pulmonary fibrosis continues to worsen. Their daughter, Princess Ingrid Alexandra is also flying back from Australia, where she is currently enrolled in university.

item.title: Laos rescuers heard ‘knocking sound’ searching cave for lost villagers
item.description: Rescue workers in Laos said they heard a knocking sound while searching a cave system for villagers that went missing after a flash flood on May 19.

item.title: Israel orders strikes on south Beirut ahead of UN meeting
item.description: Israel said Monday it would once again target Beirut's southern suburbs, a Hezbollah stronghold mostly spared heavy attacks since April, as it stages its deepest incursion into Lebanon in two decades.Beirut's southern suburbs and their surroundings have been struck twice since April 8, when huge Israeli attacks across Lebanon killed hundreds in minutes.

item.description: Several months after his arrest on suspicion of misconduct in public office and ties to Jeffrey Epstein, ex-Prince Andrew Mountbatten-Windsor has found himself under investigation again. A new report suggests that this is for his alleged inappropriate behavior towards a woman many years ago at the Royal Ascot. item.title: Frustration, anger as residents rush to flee Beirut's southern suburbs
item.description: Lebanese Army troops were deployed on Monday at the entrances to Beirut's southern suburbs to organize traffic as residents rushed to leave the area amid fears of possible Israeli strikes. Earlier Israeli Prime Minister Benjamin Netanyahu announced a decision to strike Beirut's southern suburbs, widely known as Dahiyeh, prompting anxiety among residents and disruptions to daily life.

item.title: Hondius cruise ship ready to sail again after hantavirus outbreak
item.description: The cruise ship at the centre of a hantavirus outbreak has been disinfected and is ready to set sail again with passengers, its operator said on Monday. The Hondius is to depart on June 6 for Norwegian Arctic archipelago of Svalbard.

item.title: IDF raids home of terrorist who carried out Sunday's car ramming attack in Gush Etzion
item.description: As part of the operation, soldiers from the Yehuda Brigade interrogated those close to the terrorist. The IDF overnight conducted an operation in Hebron to raid the home of the terrorist who carried out the terror ramming attack at the Gush Etzion Junction, the military announced on Monday.

item.description: Tensions have been rising in South Africa in recent weeks following several anti-migrant protests.

item.description: The French Navy conducted the operation with the support of allies including the UK on Sunday.

item.title: UK wins court case over collapsed Rwanda asylum deal
item.description: A court has ruled the UK does not need to pay Rwanda after the UK pulled a deal between the two countries.

item.title: Five patients recover from Ebola in DR Congo and leave hospital
item.description: The four nurses - three men and a woman - received certificates to celebrate their recoveries.

item.title: Fewer Syrian, Afghan, Ukrainian arrivals as migration to Germany falls
item.description: Fewer people moved to Germany last year than in 2024, with immigration down 13% to around 1. 48 million in 2025, the Federal Statistical Office in Wiesbaden reported on Monday.

item.title: US says it struck Iranian radar sites as Kuwait reports missile and drone attacks
item.description: Iran and the US say they have carried out fresh strikes around the Strait of Hormuz, with Kuwait condemning "repeated" Iranian attacks.

item.title: A secret bunker, tunnel and a Star of David tell a story of Jewish resistance in a Polish town
item.description: A secret bunker, an underground tunnel and an armband bearing the Star of David are among the rare findings in a house in southern Poland that was used by Jews, including young members of the resistance, to hide from the Nazis. “This armband is a witness, it’s like directly touching that evil which people created for other people,” Karolina Jakoweńko from the Cukerman’s Gate Foundation, which organized the search, told The Associated Press.

item.title: Confirmed Ebola cases in Congo reach 282 as survivors describe their recoveries
item.description: At least 282 confirmed cases of Ebola have now been reported in Congo’s ongoing outbreak, the central African nation said late Sunday, as patients who recovered from the disease spoke of their indescribable joy in interviews with The Associated Press. The outbreak remains focused in Congo's eastern Ituri province, where 264 of the cases have been recorded, Congo’s Ministry of Health said.

item.title: New executions in Iran following protests
item.description: Two more men have been executed in Iran in connection with the January uprising, the judiciary-linked Mizan news agency reported on Monday. Mehdad Mohammadi-Nia and Ashkan Maleki were accused of acts including setting fire to a mosque during the mass protests.

item.title: Israel PM orders strikes on Beirut suburbs as Hezbollah conflict escalates
item.description: Benjamin Netanyahu says the Hezbollah stronghold of Dahieh will be targeted in response to attacks on Israeli civilians.

item.title: Girl, 13, dies after being rescued from River Wharfe in Yorkshire
item.description: The teenager’s disappearance was reported at 6.30pm on Sunday at the River Wharfe, near Burnsall in the Yorkshire Dales

item.title: Holocaust survivor Tomi Reichental dies aged 90
item.description: Tomi Reichental, who lost 35 close family members in the Holocaust, moved to the Republic of Ireland in 1959.

item.title: Life in prison for Syrian convicted of Islamist stabbing in Germany
item.description: A Syrian man convicted of an Islamist terrorist stabbing in the west German city of Bielefeld last year was sentenced on Monday to life in prison and will remain in preventive detention if his sentence ends early due to the seriousness of his crimes, the court announced. The ruling echos the demands of federal prosecutors.

item.title: Eight killed in further clashes between Hezbollah and Israel in southern Lebanon
item.description: The conflict between Israel and the Iran-backed Lebanese Hezbollah militia escalated further, with eight people killed and 19 injured in an Israeli overnight attack in southern Lebanon, Lebanon's state news agency NNA reported on Monday. Hezbollah, a Shiite militia, has meanwhile stepped up its attacks on northern Israel.

item.title: Five killed in explosion at Hanwha Aerospace factory in S. Korea
item.description: Five workers were killed and two others injured Monday after an explosion and fire at a Hanwha Aerospace defense facility in Daejeon, officials said.

item.title: Staff-Sergeant Adam Tzarfati killed from Hezbollah drone explosion in southern Lebanon
item.description: One soldier was severely wounded, and two others were lightly wounded during the incident in which Tzarfati was killed. Staff-Sergeant Adam Tzarfati was killed in a Hezbollah drone explosion in southern Lebanon, the IDF announced on Monday.

item.title: Iran’s IRGC launches retaliatory strike after US attacks
item.description: Kuwait, which hosts US forces, says its defence systems intercepted missiles and drones amid sirens across the country.

item.title: Two Syrians accused of torture on trial in Austria
item.description: An ex-Syrian general and a former senior Syrian police officer went on trial in Vienna on Monday, accused of torturing opponents of the now-deposed regime of Bashar al-Assad.The defendants, a former brigadier general in the Syrian intelligence services and a former head of the investigations office of the local criminal police, are said to have committed the crimes in Raqa between April 2011 and March 2013.

item.description: The UK is under renewed scrutiny over support for young people entering work following a government interim report led by Alan Milburn on youth employment.

item.title: Washington proposes ‘roadmap’ for de-escalation in Lebanon: US official
item.description: Plan aims to create conducive environment for a gradual de-escalation and cessation of all hostilities, official says.

item.title: Three deaths on Irish roads over bank holiday weekend
item.description: The separate crashes occurred in County Louth, Roscommon and Dublin.

item.title: Iran claims retaliatory strike after US attack
item.description: Iran's Revolutionary Guards reported a retaliatory attack on Monday following a US airstrike. The base from which the United States had launched its attack on the southern province of Hormozgan had been struck by Iranian forces, the Islamic Revolutionary Guard Corps said in a statement.

item.title: China issues new rules to bust 'ghost' takeout deliveries
item.description: Food sellers on Chinese delivery platforms must have physical shopfronts and indicate if they do not offer dine-in services under new rules unveiled on Monday to stamp out "ghost" takeout merchants.Merchants on food delivery platforms must now have physical shopfronts and label themselves if they do not offer dine-in services, according to the SAMR's new rules.

item.title: Laos cave rescuers investigating ‘knocking response’ as focus shifts to newly discovered cave shaft
item.description: Rescuers in Laos say they are investigating a possible “knocking response” deep inside a flooded cave network where two men are believed to remain trapped.

item.title: Colombia’s Pro-Trump Presidential Candidate Pulls Off Shock Election Result
item.description: Abelardo de la Espriella, who has painted himself as a Trump supporter, will face left-wing Iván Cepeda in the runoff elections.

item.title: Philippine senator charged with plunder says he will surrender
item.description: A Philippine senator said he will surrender to authorities after a special anti-graft court ordered his arrest on Monday on a non-bailable charge of plunder after he allegedly pocketed a huge kickback in a flood-control project in the latest crisis to hit the upper chamber. The special Sandiganbayan anti-graft court had initially issued a warrant for Sen. Jinggoy Estrada’s arrest Friday on a graft charge that was bailable. Estrada, 63, has strongly denied allegations mainly by a former government public works engineer that he received more than 570 million pesos ($9.3 million) in kickbacks from flood control projects.

item.title: Many injured Ukrainians in latest Russian attacks
item.description: About a dozen people have been injured in Russia's latest attacks on Ukraine, officials in the war-torn country said on Monday. In the border city of Kharkiv in north-eastern Ukraine, two people were injured and two apartment blocks were hit, Governor Oleh Syniehubov wrote on Telegram.

item.title: Iran war day 94: US strikes Iranian sites; Kuwait intercepts missiles
item.description: While a peace deal between the US and Iran remains elusive, Israel has deepened its offensive into southern Lebanon.

item.title: U.S. and Iran trade new strikes over weekend
item.description: The United States and Iran said they traded attacks late Sunday, with each side claiming to have hit military targets, the latest tit-for-tat strikes amid a brittling cease-fire.

item.title: Vape-related fires surge 450pc as batteries explode
item.description: Vape-related fires across Britain have soared by more than 450pc as lithium batteries explode, a major insurance firm has warned.

item.title: Australia economy minister says 'legitimate' fears driving rise of far-right
item.description: Australia's economy minister said Monday that "legitimate" concerns were driving a rise in support for the far right after a bombshell opinion poll showed the populist One Nation is now the country's most popular party."I think people have legitimate concerns about where they fit in the economy," Treasurer Jim Chalmers said when asked what was driving that.

item.title: US strikes Iranian targets along Strait of Hormuz as Kuwait defends against drones, missiles
item.description: According to CENTCOM, the attacks were in response to Iran's downing of an American MQ-1 Reaper drone. The United States struck Iranian targets in Goruk and Qeshm Island, both located along the Strait of Hormuz, US Central Command (CENTCOM) announced on Monday on X/Twitter.

item.title: German court to rule on terrorist attack on football fans in Bielefeld
item.description: A German court is due to deliver its verdict on Monday in the trial of a Syrian man who has confessed to carrying out an Islamist stabbing attack that seriously wounded four people outside a bar in the western city of Bielefeld last year. Federal prosecutors have asked the Higher Regional Court in Düsseldorf to sentence the 36-year-old defendant, identified as Mahmoud M under German privacy rules, to life imprisonment for four counts of attempted murder.

item.title: Video showing sea of hajj pilgrims at Mount Arafat is AI-generated
item.description: Huge numbers of Muslims defied searing desert temperatures to pray on Mount Arafat at the climax of the hajj pilgrimage, but video purportedly showing a sea of worshippers at the site is AI-generated. The video is inconsistent with footage filmed by news organisations including AFP, and Mount Arafat's landscape, and was also flagged as synthetic by AI detection software.

item.title: Litterbugs now face on-the-spot fines in Tokyo's tourist hotspot
item.description: Dozens of officials will patrol world-famous Shibuya as Japan responds to the impacts of overtourism.

item.title: Rescuers find new access route in search for two missing Laos miners
item.description: Rescuers searching for two miners missing in a flooded cave system in Laos said they had discovered a previously unknown entrance that could provide access to the area where the men are believed to be trapped. Australian cave diver Josh Richards told dpa that he, French cave expert Robin Cuesta and soldiers found the opening after trekking through dense forest in the mountainous Xaisomboun province.

item.title: France's Macron says French Navy boarded Russia-linked oil tanker
item.description: PARIS, June 1 () - The French Navy on Sunday boarded an oil tanker, named the Tagor, which was subject ‌to international sanctions and sailing from Russia, French President Emmanuel ‌Macron wrote on X. "This operation took place in the Atlantic Ocean, on the high ​seas, with the support of several partners, including the United Kingdom, in strict compliance with the law of the sea," he said.

item.title: Returning to the fold? Some young Spaniards embrace Catholicism and can’t wait for Pope Leo’s visit
item.description: Until three years ago, Sara Cabral’s faith experience was on trend with other Southern European youth — a “Catholic but never practicing” upbringing with little relevance to her life on Spain’s Canary Islands. “You get a restlessness about an emptiness that you don’t know how to fill,” Cabral, 26, says of her embrace of Catholicism. On trips to Spain this month and France in September, Leo will find thousands of young people like her in these traditionally Catholic but now staunchly secular countries, where historic churches are abundant and Mass attendance is sparse.

item.title: Ukraine pairs MBDA Meteor missiles with Saab Gripen fighter to counter Russian glide bombs
item.description: Ukraine's air defense gap against Russian glide bombs may have an answer in the Gripen paired with the Meteor beyond-visual-range missile.

item.title: London’s deepening childlessness crisis
item.description: Having children in inner London is becoming rarer. But Jonny Singer, a 34-year-old dad in Islington who works in sports administration, is pleased that he and his wife have bucked the trend.

item.title: Iran attacks damage 20 US military sites since start of war, satellite images show
item.description: Analysts told BBC Verify that Tehran's strikes were more extensive than publicly acknowledged.

item.title: Felled tree transformed into prayer sculpture
item.description: The 175-year-old cedar had to be cut down after another tree fell during storms last year.

item.title: Brisbane begins work on the 2032 Olympics main stadium at Victoria Park after protesters moved
item.description: Protestors have been moved along and excavation machinery has been moved in as work on the main stadium for the 2032 Olympics got under way Monday, almost five years after Brisbane was awarded rights to the Summer Games. The Games Independent Infrastructure and Coordination Authority took ownership of the inner city Victoria Park site from the Brisbane City Council at midnight, and temporary perimeter fencing was erected ahead of construction on the 3.6 billion Australian dollar ($2.6 billion) Brisbane Olympics main stadium.

item.title: Polls open in Ethiopia's election that is widely expected to be won by the ruling party
item.description: Polls opened Monday in Ethiopia in an election that is widely expected to be won by the ruling party. A heavy military presence was observed in the capital, Addis Ababa, as observers called for a peaceful election in the country that is Africa's second-most populous and hosts the headquarters of the African Union. Ethiopians are choosing more than 500 members of the House of Representatives, who will subsequently vote to select the prime minister.

item.title: Police Commissioner meets with members of LGBTQ+ community ahead of Pride Month
item.description: Police and LGBTQ+ community leaders discussed security preparations as Pride Month events get underway across Israel, with the Tel Aviv Pride Parade returning to its traditional format. Police Commissioner Danny Levy met with representatives of Israel's LGBTQ+ community on Sunday, reaffirming the Israel Police's commitment to ensuring that Pride Month events throughout June are held safely and securely.

item.description: Colombia's presidential election will be decided in a run-off after right-wing lawyer Abelardo de la Espriella and left-wing Senator Iván Cepeda emerged as the top two candidates in Sunday's first-round vote. With around 99.

item.description: U.S. forces are using maneuver warfare, fragmenting ammunition and teamwork with NATO allies to develop formidable responses to drone attacks in battle.

item.title: Several haredi rioters arrested after breaking into Beit Shemesh Police station
item.description: KAN News reported on Wednesday that the IDF requested police assistance in preparation for mass arrests of haredi draft dodgers, with hundreds of officers expected to assist. Several haredi rioters were arrested on Monday after breaking into a police station in Beit Shemesh following the arrest of an alleged draft dodger, according to a Walla report.

item.title: Malaysia enforces ban on social media accounts for children younger than 16
item.description: Malaysia on Monday began enforcing rules barring millions of children younger than 16 from owning social media accounts, joining a global effort to tighten online safety protections for young users. The rules require social media platforms to implement age-verification systems and block users under 16 from creating accounts. The government said the measures are aimed at protecting children from harmful content, cyberbullying and platform features designed to encourage excessive use.

item.description: By Julia Symmes Cobb BOGOTA, May 31 (Reuters) - Colombian right wing outsider Abelardo de la Espreilla is poised to compete in a runoff in the country's presidential race against leftist senator Ivan

item.title: Ex-Prince Andrew Under Investigation For Alleged Encounter With Woman At Royal Ascot
item.description: Former prince Andrew Mountbatten-Windsor is back in the crosshairs of police in England ... he's under investigation for an alleged encounter back in the day with a woman at Royal Ascot. Cops across the pond are reportedly looking into an alleged incident from 2002 at the famous racing festival ... with police "examining events in 2002 as they consider wider possible crimes by Andrew Mountbatten-Windsor, including sexual misconduct, ­corruption and fraud" ... according to The Sunday Times. Royal Ascot is the most prestigious horse racing event in England with a rich history of tradition dating back centuries ... and in 2002, it was reportedly attended by Andrew's mom, Queen Elizabeth, his older brother the future King Charles, his younger brother Prince Edward, and his older daughter Princess Beatrice.

item.title: The drivers risking death on Ukraine's most dangerous bus routes
item.description: Russian drones are targeting public buses in Kherson, killing three transport workers so far this year.

item.description: "The others followed and deferred to her every move."
